title: NOC 95105 – Labourers in textile processing and cutting | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Canada’s textile industry is undergoing a "Green Textile" revolution, focusing on high-performance technical fabrics and sustainable garment manufacturing. Labourers in Textile Processing and Cutting (NOC 95105) are the essential support staff who manage the raw materials and initial production stages for everything from medical-grade PPE to advanced industrial filters. While classified as TEER 5, this role is a strategic entry point for international workers, offering a direct path to specialized machine operation and permanent residency through localized pilot programs in Ontario and Quebec.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Category5 (Occupations usually requiring short work demonstration)Major Group95 – Labourers in processing, manufacturing and utilitiesSub-Major Group951 – Labourers in processing and manufacturing

duties: Material Preparation: Loading and unloading textile machines with raw fibers, fabrics, or chemical dyes.
Cutting Support: Assisting cutters by spreading layers of fabric on cutting tables and marking patterns.
Finishing & Sorting: Folding, rolling, and stacking finished fabrics; sorting textile waste for recycling.
Facility Maintenance: Cleaning machines and work areas to ensure a dust-free environment, which is critical for textile quality.
2026 Update: Operating automated fabric-spreading machines and digital inventory scanners for sustainable material tracking.
employment_requirements: Education: Some secondary school education is usually required. No formal degree is necessary for entry-level roles.
Training: Short-term on-the-job training is provided by the employer.
Physical Demands: Ability to handle repetitive tasks, stand for long periods, and lift rolls of fabric (up to 20kg).
Safety: Mandatory 2026 WHMIS certification and training in the safe operation of industrial cutting tools.
job_outlook: The Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS) indicates that while automation is changing the cutting room, the "Human Touch" in quality sorting and material handling remains indispensable. Approximately 6,500 job openings are projected over the next decade, with the vast majority resulting from the retirement of the current workforce.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook for textile labourers is driven by the resurgence of domestic "On-shoring" of essential garment and technical textile production:
Quebec (Montreal): Very Good prospects. Montreal remains Canada's fashion and textile hub, with a consistent shortage of entry-level workers in the garment district.
Ontario (GTA): Good prospects. Demand is high in the technical textile sector, producing materials for the automotive and healthcare industries.
British Columbia: Stable demand. Focused on specialized outdoor apparel and marine textile manufacturing in the Lower Mainland.
National Trend: Over 65% of the current workforce is over the age of 45, leading to a massive wave of retirement openings for new international recruits.
Why Work in Canada as a Textile Labourer?
Immediate Workforce Entry: No formal post-secondary education is required, allowing you to start earning and gaining Canadian experience immediately.
Growth into Specialized Trades: Use this role as a stepping stone to become a Textile Calibration Technician or Industrial Sewing Machine Operator (NOC 94132).
Cultural Integration: Many textile hubs are located in diverse, multicultural urban centers like Montreal and Toronto.
Defined PR Pathways: Eligible for the 2026 TR to PR Pathway and specific provincial "In-Demand" streams.
pathways: 2026 TR to PR Pathway: A dedicated 2026 initiative for 33,000 temporary residents. Textile labourers with 12 months of Canadian experience are high-priority candidates.
Ontario In-Demand Skills Stream: NOC 95105 is an eligible occupation for those with a valid job offer in Ontario’s manufacturing sector.
Quebec Regular Skilled Worker Program (RSWP): An excellent pathway for those working in Montreal's vibrant textile and fashion sector.
Saskatchewan Hard-to-Fill Skills Pilot: A streamlined pathway for semi-skilled workers with a job offer in the province’s manufacturing sector.
